Name: kernel_based_hybrid_quantization
Title: Kernel-Based Hybrid Quantization for High-Throughput Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: This research proposes a hybrid quantization approach that combines both layer-wise adaptive mixed precision and kernel-level optimization to achieve higher throughput and lower latency in inference, specifically tailored for commodity GPUs. The need arises from the limitations of existing quantization methods that fail to exploit the full range of hardware capabilities and the empirical performance that can be achieved through targeted kernel optimizations.
Related Work: Existing works like AMP-ViT and SNIP focus on adaptive mixed precision but typically do not integrate kernel-level optimizations that are crucial for practical deployment on commodity GPUs. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly linking quantization strategies with hardware profiling to tune kernel execution in real-time, leveraging the empirical evaluation paradigm.
Abstract: This proposal introduces Kernel-Based Hybrid Quantization (KBHQ), a novel approach that integrates adaptive mixed precision quantization with rigorous kernel-level optimization strategies to maximize inference throughput and minimize latency on commodity GPUs. By utilizing a profiling-driven framework, KBHQ dynamically adjusts the bit-width of model parameters based on the current hardware usage and performance metrics, such as memory bandwidth and kernel occupancy. This real-time adjustment is coupled with a suite of optimized CUDA kernels that are specifically tuned for the quantized data formats, ensuring that the compute resources are fully utilized. We will empirically validate the efficacy of KBHQ through comprehensive benchmarking across various model architectures, reporting metrics such as p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy consumption per token. The anticipated results are expected to demonstrate significant speedups over existing state-of-the-art quantization methods while maintaining model accuracy.
Experiments: Kernel Profiling: Measure wall-clock latency and throughput for various configurations of mixed precision on commodity GPUs.
Quantization Strategy Testing: Implement KBHQ on a set of standard models (e.g., ResNet, ViT) and compare against traditional mixed-precision methods.
Dynamic Adjustment Evaluation: Test the adaptive mechanism's performance by varying load conditions and assessing the adjustments in precision and kernel execution.
Performance Metrics: Evaluate p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token across different configurations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include limited generalizability across different GPU architectures and potential overhead associated with real-time profiling. Furthermore, the performance of the hybrid approach may be sensitive to the specific models being tested, and extensive validation across a wide range of architectures may be necessary.
